title: Castling
summary: Move king and rook in a single step

steps.title: What Is Castling?
steps.text:
Castling is the **only move in chess** where two pieces move at once — your king and one of your rooks, on the same turn.

It does two priceless things in a single tempo:

- **Hides the king** in a corner, tucked behind a wall of pawns
- **Activates a rook**, dropping it onto a central or semi-open file

## Two flavors

- **Kingside castle (short)**: written {{O-O}}. King hops two squares toward the h-rook; the rook lands next to him.
- **Queenside castle (long)**: written {{O-O-O}}. King hops two squares toward the a-rook; the rook flies over him to the other side.

In the position above, both sides have set up perfectly to castle either way. Imagine playing {{O-O}} — your king is suddenly safe, your rook on f1 eyes the e-file.

> Castling is two great moves wrapped into one. Don't waste it.

steps.title: The Four Rules
steps.text:
Castling looks magical, but it has strict requirements. Forget any of them and the move is illegal.

## The four conditions

1. **Neither piece has moved.** The king and the chosen rook must both be on their original squares, untouched.
2. **The squares between them are empty.** No bishop or knight blocking the path.
3. **The king is not in check.** You can't castle to escape a check.
4. **The king doesn't pass through or land on an attacked square.** The transit squares must be safe too.

A classic mnemonic: *can't castle into, out of, or through check*.

Notice that the **rook** is allowed to be attacked or to pass through an attacked square — only the king's path matters. That's a subtle but useful detail in queenside castling.

> If you're not sure you can castle, double-check those four rules. The engine never forgives an illegal move.

steps.prompt: Castle kingside with **{{O-O}}** — king to g1, rook to f1, both safe in one move.
steps.hint: In most chess interfaces you simply move the king two squares toward the h-file rook — the rook follows automatically.

steps.prompt: Now Black castles long with **{{O-O-O}}** — king to c8, rook to d8, attacking down the d-file.
steps.hint: Move the king two squares toward the a-file rook. The rook hops over to d8 in the same motion.

steps.title: Both Kings Tucked In
steps.text:
Both sides have castled — White short, Black long — and the position now has its full character.

Notice what changed:

- **Both kings are off the e-file.** No more accidental pins or pokes from a queen.
- **All four rooks are connected** along their back ranks. They can swing to any open file.
- **Both armies are battle-ready.** The fight for the center can begin.

When you castle on **opposite sides** (one player short, one long), watch out — pawn storms become deadly because each side can hurl pawns at the other king without exposing his own. A racing attack often decides the game.

> Aim to castle by move 10. Late castles get caught in the middle of the board.
